bibliographic.citation: E. (Erich) Adam, ed., Torrent of Portyngale: Re-edited from the Unique MS. in the Chetham Library, Manchester, EETS Extra Series 51 (London: published for the Early English Text Society by N. Trübner & Co., 1887), xxxiv + 120 pp.
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 51
bibliographic.title: Torrent of Portyngale: Re-edited from the Unique MS. in the Chetham Library, Manchester

entities.label: Erich Adam's 1887 Torrent of Portyngale
notes: This is the immediate printed source represented by CME00056.
The edited poem occupies pp. 1-92; seven early-print fragments occupy pp. 93-100; notes, glossary, and index occupy pp. 101-120.

Adam says he entered most conjectural restorations into the text, retained manuscript orthography as accurately as possible, expanded contractions in italics, and used the print fragments in difficult passages.

Adam states that Eugen Kölbing revised the introduction, notes, and glossary before and after printing and repeatedly proofread the text; Joseph Hall also checked proofs against the Chetham manuscript.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1887; Internet Archive metadata reports NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.
rights.component: Adam's 1887 edition text and apparatus

bibliographic.citation: E. Adam, Torrent of Portyngale, EETS ES 51 (1887; unaltered reprint, Millwood, NY: Kraus Reprint Co., 1973).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 51
bibliographic.title: Torrent of Portyngale

dateStatements.label: reprint publication
dateStatements.notes: The added Kraus title leaf says Millwood, New York, 1973 and the verso calls the volume an unaltered reprint produced with EETS permission.

entities.label: Kraus 1973 unaltered reprint of Adam's 1887 edition
notes: The cached Internet Archive PDF scans this reprint, not a copy lacking the 1973 Kraus leaves; the inherited 1887 text and the added reprint matter are kept distinct for rights purposes.

rights.basis: The scan contains added 1973 Kraus matter even though Internet Archive describes the item as 1887 and reports NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT; no item-specific permission for the added matter was found.
rights.component: 1973 Kraus title leaves, reprint presentation, and any edition-specific matter
notes: The public-domain 1887 content and the later reprint wrapper are separate rights layers.

bibliographic.citation: James Orchard Halliwell, ed., Torrent of Portugal: An English Metrical Romance, Now First Published from an Unique Manuscript of the Fifteenth Century, Preserved in the Chetham Library at Manchester (London: John Russell Smith, 1842), xii + 120 pp.
bibliographic.title: Torrent of Portugal: An English Metrical Romance

entities.label: James Orchard Halliwell's 1842 Torrent of Portugal
notes: The first complete modern publication, transcribed from the Chetham manuscript; its appendix prints six early-print fragments from the Bodleian Douce collection.
Halliwell acknowledges C. G. Hulton's assistance during transcription and use of Sir Frederic Madden's independent 1835 transcript.
Its line numbering accidentally jumps from 1720 to 1820; Adam preserves Halliwell-equivalent numbers in brackets after line 1200 to aid comparison.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1842; Internet Archive metadata reports NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.
rights.component: Halliwell's 1842 edition text and apparatus

access.accessMethod: Use Manchester Imaging Services to request an authorized complete-object surrogate; specify every manuscript leaf, covers/boards, inner covers and pastedowns, endpapers, flyleaves, blanks, spine, and edges where offered, and identify fols. 76r-119v separately as the work portion
access.contact: University of Manchester Library Imaging Services; quote Chetham's Library MS Mun.A.6.31 (formerly MS 8009)
access.format: Repository-supplied digital images; requested delivery and publication permissions depend on quotation
notes: The existing viewer already publishes 768 surfaces, but an authorized retained whole-object delivery is needed before a local cache can be recorded.

Ask separately about private research storage, repository publication, commercial print-on-demand use, attribution, and whether the requested delivery may include or redistribute images not covered by the collection-level CC statement.

entities.description: The unique near-complete manuscript witness of Sir Torrent, copied on paper in a late-fifteenth-century miscellany. Torrent occupies fols. 76r-119v; the final surface is shared with the opening of the Lamentation of the Virgin.

holding.notes: The LUNA records give current repository Chetham's Library, Manchester and previous accession number 8009.

entities.label: Chetham's Library MS Mun.A.6.31

physicalDescription.format: Late-fifteenth-century household miscellany; Torrent is written by two hands and has rubricated initials but no separately spaced stanzas
notes: The LUNA parent sequence comprises 768 digital surfaces from upper cover through lower cover, including inner covers, endpapers, flyleaves, blanks, and adjacent works.
Modern analysis assigns Torrent lines 1-1049 to Hand 7 and the remainder to Hand 5.
The final Torrent lines and the following Lamentation of the Virgin share fol. 119v.

rights.basis: The anonymous work and fifteenth-century witness predate modern copyright; image and access conditions are analyzed separately.
rights.component: Medieval Sir Torrent textual content

access.accessMethod: Browse the complete 768-record LUNA sequence; use the exact beginning and ending work records for fols. 76r and 119v
access.entityLabel: Manchester LUNA published sequence for Chetham MS Mun.A.6.31
access.format: 768 color image records; one single-image IIIF manifest per surface
notes: Coverage audit: image 1 upper cover; 2 inner upper cover; 3-6 endpapers; 7-10 flyleaves; 163 fol. 76r; 250 fol. 119v; 759-762 flyleaves; 763-766 endpapers; 767 inner lower cover; 768 lower cover.
Sir Torrent occupies images 163-250. Fol. 119v is shared with the following Lamentation of the Virgin.
No separate spine or edge images were found.
No local cache was made because the governing site terms prohibit systematic extraction or reutilization of substantial parts.

rights.basis: Manchester's support page says University of Manchester images are released under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, but warns that some collections have different restrictions and directs users to item Rights Holder fields; these records name Chetham's Library as rights holder for image and work.

rights.basis: Site terms allow personal, non-commercial extracts but prohibit systematic extraction or reutilization of substantial parts, commercial use without license, and publication of a database containing substantial parts.
rights.component: Contractual use of Manchester Digital Collections content and database
notes: These terms are why no whole-manuscript image bundle was cached despite technical image availability.

entities.description: A publicly viewable 768-image color sequence covering the repository's published inventory from upper cover through lower cover. It includes inner covers, endpapers, flyleaves, blanks, all manuscript leaves, and adjacent works; no separate spine or edge photographs were found.

bibliographic.citation: James Wade, ed., Sir Torrent of Portingale (Kalamazoo, MI: Medieval Institute Publications, 2017), Middle English Texts Series.
bibliographic.title: Sir Torrent of Portingale

entities.label: James Wade's 2017 Sir Torrent of Portingale
notes: A later comparison edition, not an ancestor of CME00056.
Wade follows the Chetham manuscript as copy-text, adopts print-fragment readings when strongly supported by rhyme, meter, or line order, and documents emendations in textual notes.
The cached 127-page PDF preserves the complete edition: introduction, 2,671-line text, explanatory and textual notes, bibliography, and glossary.

next_steps: Request the complete physical manuscript rather than fols. 76r-119v alone and identify that folio range separately as the work portion.
Ask whether the delivery may be stored in the private Git-ignored cache and whether any images may later be published in the repository or used in commercial print-on-demand research outputs.
If authorized files arrive, preserve every delivered surface, record exact source URLs or order identifiers, hash the files, and add a complete or explicitly qualified local_copies record.
notes: The public viewer is complete relative to its 768-surface inventory, but current anti-extraction terms prevent bulk caching; no spine or edge photographs are exposed.
openQuestions.question: Will Chetham's Library or Manchester Imaging Services supply an authorized retained complete-object surrogate of Mun.A.6.31, including every leaf and all covers, boards, spine, pastedowns, endpapers, flyleaves, blanks, and edges where available, with explicit research and publication terms?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections to confirm the current shelfmark, printer attribution, and complete recto/verso inventory for each two-leaf object.
Compare authorized whole-object images against Adam pp. 93-100 and Wade's F.I-F.VII textual notes.
Update the two fragment relations with exact object-specific passage ranges once the crosswalk is verified.
notes: The current manifest deliberately does not invent which of Adam's seven fragments belongs to which printed object.
openQuestions.question: How do the current physical SOLO record, Wade's e.20(1)/e.20(2) bibliography, STC 24133/24133.5, and Adam's seven numbered fragment groups map object by object?

next_steps: Request written clarification from Manchester Imaging Services and Chetham's Library for this exact shelfmark.
Ask separately about private research storage, non-commercial Git publication, and commercial or print-on-demand use.
notes: Until clarified, no whole sequence should be downloaded or redistributed from the public viewer.
openQuestions.question: Does the collection-level CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 statement govern the Chetham images despite item fields naming Chetham's Library as rights holder and site terms restricting systematic extraction and transmission?

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local representation of University of Michigan CME item CME00056, manually keyed from Erich Adam's 1887 EETS edition. The XML includes Adam's title page, edited reading text (printed pp. 1-92), and seven early-print fragments (pp. 93-100), but omits his introduction, notes, glossary, and index. Adam retranscribed the unique near-complete manuscript, Chetham's Library Mun.A.6.31, fols. 76r-119v, and corrected it with two independent early-sixteenth-century Bodleian print fragments, Halliwell's 1842 edition, and Kölbing's 1884 collation. Complete printed volumes and later comparison editions are cached with exact internal mappings. Manchester exposes all 768 published manuscript surfaces, including covers and endleaves, but its anti-systematic-extraction terms prevent a compliant bulk cache; the complete viewer, exact work range, and authorized whole-object reproduction route are recorded instead.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Toward the end of the fourteenth century (often c. 1400); uniquely attested as a near-complete text in a manuscript copied c. 1470-1480; fragmentarily printed c. 1505 and c. 1510
catalogSummary.region: England; probably the North Midlands, with a more specific northern East Midlands localization proposed but not certain
catalogSummary.title: Sir Torrent of Portingale

catalogingSubject.label: Sir Torrent of Portingale
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English tail-rhyme romance conventionally titled Sir Torrent of Portingale. The intellectual work is distinct from its unique near-complete Chetham manuscript, two independent early-print fragments, Adam's edited reading text and parallel fragment appendix, later scholarly editions, digital encodings, repository XML, and photographic reproductions. Adam's seven fragment groups are alternate witnesses to passages of this same work, not seven intrinsic continuations or chapters.

contentParts.label: Sir Torrent of Portingale

locators.value: 76r-119v; fol. 119v shared with the following Lamentation of the Virgin
163-250; image 250 is the shared fol. 119v boundary
pp. 1-92 edited reading text; pp. 93-100 seven parallel early-print fragments
BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem'] plus BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='addendum']; the addendum is parallel fragment evidence, not narrative continuation

notes: The manuscript does not space the twelve-line stanzas separately, and damage leaves many stanzas incomplete.

Adam's seven fragment groups cover line ranges 462-491, 492-521, 819-851, 917-947, 950-971, 1014-1037, and 1807-1866. They are alternate print witnesses and therefore are not modeled as intrinsic content parts.
Folio 119v remains within the work mapping because it contains Torrent's ending even though the following Lamentation begins on the same surface.
contentParts.summary: A continuous tail-rhyme romance tracing Torrent's quests for Desonell, separation from his wife and twin sons, crusading adventures and unrecognized family encounters, recognition and reunion, marriage, dynastic settlement, and imperial elevation.

assertion.notes: The date is a scholarly estimate, not a dated authorial or scribal statement.

dateStatements.display: Toward the end of the fourteenth century; often cataloged c. 1400

assertion.notes: The repository broadly dates the codex to the fifteenth century; the narrower 1470-1480 range is current scholarly cataloging.

dateStatements.display: 1470-1480 (often c. 1475) in Chetham's Library MS Mun.A.6.31

assertion.notes: The date and attribution are conventional and the surviving object is fragmentary.

dateStatements.display: c. 1505? in a fragmentary Richard Pynson printing

scope.description: Earliest surviving print witness; only two leaves survive

assertion.notes: This does not erase the earlier fragmentary print witnesses.

dateStatements.display: 1842, first complete modern publication by James Orchard Halliwell

extent.description: Wade's current critical edition presents 2,671 lines of the romance; the Database of Middle English Romance reports 2,668 lines for the manuscript text. The CME main-poem division contains 2,677 L elements grouped in 236 LG elements, while its seven-fragment addendum contains a further 234 L elements in 7 LG groups. Those XML figures are representation metrics: the addendum duplicates selected passages from independent early prints, and main-text L nodes do not map one-to-one to every scholarly lineation. The unique near-complete manuscript text occupies fols. 76r-119v. No canonical stanza total is asserted because the transmitted poem is damaged and incomplete in many stanzas.

formStatements.description: Narrative verse in twelve-line tail-rhyme stanzas, conventionally represented as aab ccb ddb eeb, with metrical damage and missing lines in the surviving witnesses.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English introductions, notes, and glosses belong to editorial editions and are not additional languages of the medieval work.
The catalog summary's editor names the immediate CME source editor and his extensively credited collaborator; it does not imply joint medieval authorship.

Adam's conjecture of a lost older English poem common to Torrent and Eglamour is retained only as historical editorial interpretation. Current metadata states the supported, narrower claim that Eglamour is the closest analogue and likely partial source.

The Chetham witness is the unique near-complete manuscript, but two detached early-print objects preserve overlapping passages from a separate transmission branch; 'unique witness' is therefore qualified rather than absolute.

next_steps: Build a line-level crosswalk from Adam's printed number milestones, Halliwell bracket numbers, Wade's lineation, and XML L boundaries.
Keep the 234 addendum L elements separate because they duplicate selected early-print passages rather than extending the narrative.
openQuestions.question: How do Wade's 2,671-line edition, the Database of Middle English Romance's 2,668 manuscript lines, Adam's historical numbering and Halliwell cross-numbers, and the CME main division's 2,677 L elements align line by line?

next_steps: Consult Montgomery's full dialect study and subsequent linguistic scholarship.
Retain broad and narrow localization claims as separately qualified assertions until authorial and scribal layers can be distinguished.
openQuestions.question: Can the broad North Midlands composition localization be narrowed securely without confusing authorial dialect, scribal dialect, and the production place of the surviving Chetham copy?

next_steps: Obtain or inspect complete recto-verso images of both two-leaf objects.
Reconcile Wade's bibliography with the current SOLO physical record and map every printed group to its source object.
notes: Until resolved, the content model treats the seven groups as parallel witness evidence without assigning them to one physical print object.
openQuestions.question: Which of Adam's seven fragment groups belongs to the Pynson object and which to the de Worde object under current Bodleian shelfmarks?

assertion.notes: A scholarly localization based on dialect and linguistic evidence, not a documentary place of writing.
Wade reports the narrower localization as a scholarly proposal; it should not be converted into a certain settlement-level origin.

placeStatements.placeLabel: Northern East Midlands near the Lincolnshire-Nottinghamshire-West Riding intersection

subjects.label: Quests, combat, and monsters
notes: Includes repeated combats with giants, a dragon, and animal dangers; these are central plot structures rather than incidental motifs.

subjects.label: Family separation, exile, recognition, and reunion
Sea voyages, exile, and crusade travel

summaries.value: Torrent, an earl's son in Portugal, loves Princess Desonell, but her father repeatedly tries to prevent the match by sending him on lethal quests. Torrent defeats giants, a dragon, and rival champions across an expansive romance geography. After Desonell bears twins, her father casts mother and children to sea; the boys are separately rescued and raised in Jerusalem and Greece, while Desonell reaches Nazareth. Torrent returns, joins crusading warfare, encounters his sons without recognizing them, and ultimately reunites with his family through tournaments, stories, rings, and other tokens of recognition. The lovers marry, their sons inherit kingdoms, and Torrent is elected emperor.

This record catalogs the medieval romance, not Adam's editorial introduction or apparatus. The main CME poem division and its seven-fragment addendum are two editorial representations of overlapping work passages. The fragment appendix is retained in lineage and source mappings but is not counted as additional narrative continuation.

notes: The preferred catalog title follows a current scholarly edition; spelling varies substantially across manuscript and editions.
titles.supplied_by: James Wade's current Middle English Texts Series edition
Erich Adam's 1887 edition and the CME source title page

notes: Line division is retained to distinguish the two-line opening rubric from a modern supplied title.
titles.value: Here bygynneth a good tale / Of Torrente of Portyngale

work.summary: Torrent, an earl's son in Portugal, loves Princess Desonell, but her father repeatedly tries to prevent the match by sending him on lethal quests. Torrent defeats giants, a dragon, and rival champions across an expansive romance geography. After Desonell bears twins, her father casts mother and children to sea; the boys are separately rescued and raised in Jerusalem and Greece, while Desonell reaches Nazareth. Torrent returns, joins crusading warfare, encounters his sons without recognizing them, and ultimately reunites with his family through tournaments, stories, rings, and other tokens of recognition. The lovers marry, their sons inherit kingdoms, and Torrent is elected emperor.
